column36: Comments

I tried a "safe" choice for my first game. Deathlok could send Shield heroes undercover from the KO pile, so if I kept one hand as all Shield, then I thought I only had to worry about the Scheme for my other hand. I brought Coulson along to lean into Undercover a bit more. I'd already used Mockingbird in another round. My strategy worked, but it ramped up very slowly. My uncommon Coulson card only managed a 9 attack at his best, and my non-Shield hand ended up doing more of the work with Dark Memories. I won on turn 36, with 1 escape, and my only card left in the Villain Deck a Twist.

I thought the first game was boring, and wouldn't go better with a second try, so I switched to Iceman and Wolverine. A blue/red hand and a yellow/red hand, with the blue side being all Iceman and the yellow side trying to make Wolverine and Elektra work. I never bought into the Iceman hype, but I get it now. He works well with Bishop as well. My Iceman hand ended the game after generating 66 attack. My Wolverine/Elektra hand was clearing the city, but didn't get into big numbers. I saw 7 Twists this game, but they were mostly early game before I had KO'd a lot of cards, so it didn't snipe very many cards.

We went through several scheme twists early and were able to survive with 5 non-grey escapes. Player 1 had 2 big hands with dark memories.  Player 2 with a Iceman, Bishop and Photon air (blue) machine got a 50 attack last hand to wipe the board.

column32: I did not make a second attempt.
column33: I did not make a second attempt.
column36: Tough one, got 4 Master Strikes early which made the Rings really hard to manage. In hindsight I probably should've accepted a few escapes to fight Taskmaster sooner, but still won in the end.
We struggled to get going and the first couple of twists KOed vital cards, causing us both to pivot our plans for our decks
Melk

We had 4 twists in the first 5 turns of game 1, which helped set us up for a good run. I managed to turn 4/2 into 5/1 on both of my first two goes, which was nice! My wife went for Bishop/Deathlok and thinned her deck, I had Hellcat and F/WS for lots of draw. Neither of us picked up a card worth more than 5, so we only needed to hit 10 for the Mastermind. We also massively abused the Ring that discards 0 cost heroes from your deck late on, playing itany many times. I never got to Reanimate to reduce the Evil Wins, mostly as Deathlok wasn't ever sniped, and I never saw Hellcat's rare which was my other plan to keep things going. Overall a challenge but not too bad due to our shuffle
Tough setup, but fun. I don't think I found the right combo of heroes, though it was fun trying to find the perfect combo.

We were barely surviving game one with cable helping us hit mastermind. However the hq got so expensive and we didn’t get colossus common until near end of game. Second game we tried to lean into hyper speed and got Mercd by a scheme twist and early master strikes leading to escapes and being unable to keep the city clear.

cable worked as intended against the mm. had a lot of turns where I was one attack short to it the beefed up henchmen so a couple escaped. I attribute my win to pure luck. I don't like this scheme since successful snipe is so consequential compared to an unsuccessful one.

game 1 was so close to be a way better score but last turn put multiple villains into the city pushing me into 2 escapes which also put the colored K.O. count to 7(dodged a bullet here)!!! 
expected more from iceman tbh and tried to make it work but also didnt swap it out for game 2 since i already had the win under my belt, attempt 2 went worse even though it was also a win.
Darkhawk did well with War Machine and other black cards from Blade and Black Widow. Player 2 just kept HQ clean and did not do much.

column36: Brutal setup. Scheme plus Mastermind and Villains made for a tough and not so fun first game. Switched up the heroes and made all the difference as did a much luckier card draw from Villain deck. If I had to play the scheme again (which I won't by choice) would have chosen Green/Yellow heroes as they were the first collected for the "Zoo." I feel if I played this another 10 times, I'd probably lose each time. Just a really luck based scheme.

A decently difficult setup. This Scheme had its imprint in my head that it was very hard, and my games sort of confirmed that. I tried to play around the first 5 Scheme Twists, which was annoying but succesful in attempt 1 and pointless and unseccesful in attempt 2. Early Schemes are nice, so you can recruit more colours safely, but the shuffle was merciless on attempt 2. Not a lot of early Scheme Twists and a city filled with very strong enemies. Mandarin's Rings are a cool Henchmen Group, but their 4 attack instead of 3 was a real challenge, especially in attempt 2.

I chose Werewolf by Night and Elsa Bloodstone as my heroes in the hope that they would have a lot of synergy and that after two schemes most of their cards would be safe to get. In attempt 1 the 'draw a card for every Mandarin ring' tactic was the last one and it was defeated by the deck that had 5 rings in the Victory Pile, I only had 3 cards left in my deck and discard combined, but Elsa's rare was one of the drawn cards, which allowed me to also hit the Final Blow and the Tactic that became a Location. I then noticed that Frigga, Mother of Thor was still in the City, delaying my victory by 2 turns.

Deck 2 was built around Tech cards in both games, but it was the weaker deck in attempt 1 and the stronger deck in attempt 2. Deck 1 was a good blend of (among others) Werewolf by Night's Strength Commons (recruited just after a very early Scheme Twist), Blade, Daywalker's Instinct Uncommon and some of Elsa Bloodstone's Tech Commons. Werewolf by Night and Blade, Daywalker were very effective in KO-ing starters and the occasional wound, but at one point I had no recruit left in deck 1. I doubled down on that and generated between 10 and 26 attack per turn depending on what cards I drew. I could get Blade, Daywalker's Rare from a Party Skrull and Elsa Bloodstone's Rare from an Opera Singer while I still had some recruit left in my Deck.

Attempt 2 gave so many early wounds from Master Strikes and Rampages that it would have been a feat just to win, but after 7 twists that game was lost. In Attempt 1 only 3 Heroes were collected for the Interstellar Zoo, so not too worrisome. 

Thanks to Kylah for another fun setup, Mandarin's Rings do have some KO and depending on the master strikes coming after the rings you could choose to send them back to the City and defeat them again. The forced Heroes were a bit of a jumble. Siryn and War Machine were a little anti synergistic with the Scheme and Mastermind, but I guess that was by design; anti synergy is Kylah's signature for making harder setups and making sure all colours were present in the Hero Deck was a way to make sure the Scheme could not be 'cheated' . Getting Mandarin weaker by getting his rings was the safe but sure way to win, and I got lucky that I had enough time to finish that way in attempt 1. Attempt 2 was drastically different, but that's the shuffle randomness in action.

column36: Heroes work nicely together.  Scheme wasn't very interesting, though it did make me lose a few cards.  Hyperspeed was pretty cool.

Oh my goodness! This was extremely difficult! We got soooo close to a win in our first game but ultimately lost after 36 turns! The second game went better but was still very challenging and really could have ended with a loss on a scheme twist pull for the last 4 turns (thank goodness we were able to hit the MM one last time before a scheme twist was revealed). "M" really helped a lot in this matchup and so did Scarlet Witch (3 cost cards for the win!). M's basic card also helped clean up our hands of the many wounds being dealt to us by the set up. Both games we managed to get War Machine's rare which was very helpful. A really challenging set up that I feel fortunate to have actually won....but felt luck really helped us out on the second match. oof! thanks for the spanking Kylah! :-)

column36: I was lucky to have a LOT of scheme twists early in the game so they whiffed. It was a very close win, I was able to defeat Frigga (After I defeated The Mandarin) thanks to The Nightbringer Ring that allowed me to put a Master strike on top of the Scheme Twist that would have caused me to loose.

Not my favorite Scheme, but at least it forced me to use a Hero I dislike whose best Common effect works antithetically to the Mastermind.

Four Strikes by turn 10, and didn't see more than one Henchman until about turn 20, so lots of Wounds and a long-swollen Mastermind.  Things were looking dire, so I chanced my first hit on the Mandarin when the other hand didn't have tech, and lost that 1-in-4 gamble so had an escape.  

I did manage to not lose a single Hero card to the first 5 Twists, so at least that bought me the time for things to otherwise come together for a Win.  The second game wasn't so lucky.

column36: After few tournaments you learn to expect obligatory Deadlands stage, and you learn the mantra "put bystanders on top of pile to cover zombies" and Viv Vision is very good at putting bystanders

We both had fun with this set up. Ms Marvel did us well in the first game though the villain deck didn't. In our 2nd game, another win with only one more turn taken, we didn't see her as much. We weren't such a fan of Karnak though. Great set up thanks.

Fun setup! The setup heroes gave a good avenue for managing Zombie Green Goblin (Wolfsbane managing the HQ), and I felt good about my hero selections to manage other parts of the setup. I prioritized heroes which could rescue Bystanders to manage Rise of the Living Dead and heroes with low printed attack to manage Abomination. My hero choices of Ms. Marvel and Nova were great for those goals! This setup proved to be a great one for showcasing the fun and power of the Champions expansion - one which I was excited to play as I had not done much of previously. Ms. Marvel was an all-star! Cheering Crowds was a fun and unique way to get utility from the Bystanders which protected me from Rise earlier in the game. Ms. Marvel is now solidly one of my favorite heroes in the game. My first time playing Wolfsbane, and it really impressed me as well. Satisfying and clever setup that rewarded good hero selections to manage some otherwise intimidating Villain Groups.

Decided to go all in on champions for the bystanders to bury the RotLD. Surprise MVP was Karnak for his uncommon regularly giving 4+ attack. LVP goes to Nova for getting his rare KOd twice as the only rare KO. Also using wolfsbane and the inhuman rebellion to keep rares out of the HQ when I wasn't about to imminently recruit them.

I have never played this scheme before. Was really fun, Zombie GG could be really annoying depending on your hero deck. We had two really different games but more lucky on the first one. My heroes selection were more defensive and that made a game to be too long but at least kept the city easy to control.
We were able to get Karnak's rare REALLY early, and were able to hit mastermind every few turns.  First attempt was brutal, getting Living Deaded to death.

I really loved this setup! It took me a long time to settle on the strategy I wanted to pursue; for a while I was pursuing Green Heroes trying to make a Hulk thing work, or I wanted to see if I could use the color variety to make Crystal happen, but I the tried and true APK+Maximus combo looked really good here and I couldn't turn it down. Maximus Maximused and APK popped off in combination with Karnak on the other side and man I just had a blast with it. I was pleasantly surprised by how Karnak worked, I've overlooked him before and I'm excited to try him more often now!

In the first attempt I managed to get a good number of Wound management cards and make good use of them, in the second I saw hardly any but got more Henchmen early on and Elsa and Wolfie did lots of hunting for Bystanders. 2nd attempt only took longer because a Fenris Ambush parked a Infinity Gem Villain with a Villainous Weapon and shards totalling up to 15 Attack on the Streets for me. Took forever to get enough Attack to clear it. Hela’s Tactic that pulls from the Villain deck also grabbed a hefty gem and cost me more turns. At least I never saw the Time Gem or the second Fenris! I was hoping to get lots of KOs from Snarling Fangs but that never really kicked off in either game. I got a lot more use out of Prune the Growths and Okoye’s dual-class common. Elsa’s cards were valuable in both games, triggering off of Gritty Scavenger on Instinct cards. Probably should have just used Star-Lord, but I was worried about escapes while getting him set up.

In game 2 it was even more unlucky, between the both of us we missed "tracking Hank down" 6 times where we could not pay the difference literally by 1 attack. Ended up turning in the game with better points per turn and won faster despite the single escape.

Its always interesting playing a game in which you want to KO your starters quickly but then you need them to fight some of the villains as well. I don't believe that I did overall very well but additionally I don't feel like it was a poor performance. I did enjoy the set up and the game play just don't feel great about the results of the games.

Every time I play against Hank it makes me wonder why I even got What If in the first place.

Unlucky rotation in HQ🥲😁

I love the idea you had going here Kylah, but a timer scheme the could be over on twist 5 with an Omega level mutant as a mastermind did not turn out so well. I know making set-ups are hard, I really did enjoy building my decks, but I really wish  schemes like this one and organized crime wave didn't exist. I love the idea and theming, but I wished they were more bearable. An example of this is changing the ambush for the Magia Goons from: play a card from the villain deck to: reveal the top card of the villain deck, if it's not a scheme twist play it.

The combined force of Shield and half the Fantastic Four along with Shatterstar tried their best to stop Exodus from uniting the shards, but unfortunately, he could not be stopped. Only delayed. We were the ones shattered in the end.

column36: At first this set-up felt like it pigeonholed your hero choices, but the longer I think about it the more I’m concluding that that is part of the challenge. The setup is telling you to go into one of five or six different directions and you have to decide which ones, if any, to go into. I do like this type of scheme, where the scheme twists don’t drastically change the board state and instead they only escalate the danger. At first I regretted not bringing Domino, but after getting Darkhawk's rare early, F4U's Focus really helped to turn the extra recruit into additional attack. P2 was trying to do a draw deck, but the wrong Iron Fist common was gumming up the HQ for it to really take off. In the end I don't think it mattered and P1 was on fire most turns. I did get real lucky with some early Twist KOs from an empty city, but never really felt too worried.
Fun game. Luckily we were able to contain the city enough where Weaponized Galactus hardly posed any threat.

Great fun in this setup!  Hand 1 got a few Rare's early on, but many early master strikes made Stryfe formidable and really hurt Hand 2 overall.  Deathlok and War Machine with Captain Marvel and Darkhawk was a great combo.  Iron Fist never hurts any game either.  Not having X-force heroes only really hurt hand 2, who still performed.  Great fun.

The mastermind wasn't oppressive and the scheme was threatening but never felt impossible to deal with. We got off the ground a bit slow but when we got going, the game was over very quickly.

Setup had some nice tension, our first game the city was blown up quickly and literally by Galactus. Got much more manageable villain deck the second go round where we actually saw some Hydra bases and plutonium and were able to deal with things and KO while we scaled. Chose Iceman to synergize with Captain Marvel and Cable to counter master-strikes and his Mastermind power-up. Expect some will still be able to have finished it faster but very happy with our second effort compared to the first time of what could go wrong, did.
